text:
Prediction: by 2026, your API's #1 consumer won't be a developer. It will be an LLM deciding at runtime whether your endpoint is worth invoking.

That changes what "API-first" means.

Old definition: design for developers writing curl commands.
New definition: design for agents reading your spec and choosing your tool over a competitor's.

The shift is already measurable. Gartner projects 30%+ of new API demand by 2026 will come from AI consumption. The latest State of the API benchmark put AI/LLM integration as the top driver of new API investment, displacing internal dev productivity for the first time.

Here's the Agent Experience (AX) breakdown PMs should run against every endpoint:

1. **Discoverability** — Is your OpenAPI spec semantically rich enough that an agent knows when to call you vs. a competitor?

2. **Description quality** — Every endpoint and parameter needs natural-language descriptions written for a model, not a human skimmer.

3. **Typed, structured errors** — Agents retry on signal. Vague 400s get abandoned; typed errors with recovery hints get re-invoked.

4. **Idempotency and safety** — Agents will call your endpoint 50 times. Design for it.

5. **Tool-spec compatibility** — MCP-style machine-readable contracts are becoming table stakes across every major model provider.

The step everyone skips: **error semantics.** Teams polish happy paths and ship 400s that read like internal tickets.

The best teams now treat their OpenAPI spec as core product surface area, reviewed with the same rigor as the UI.

If your API isn't legible to an agent in 2026, you're invisible in the AI distribution layer.

For the PMs reading: who owns AX on your team today? For the execs: is it in your roadmap, or still buried under "develop
